RAy! MEH1A
CHAIR I AX
FAIR
POLITICAL PRACTICES CoMNlIssIoN
April
15,
1996
J.
Sal Munoz
p.
0.
Box 890158
Teinecula,
CA 92589
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1—96—108
Dear Mr.
Munoz:
You have requested advice concerning the conflict of interest
disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
!
Because your inquiry is general in nature,
we will treat your
letter as
a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c).
2
!
QUESTION
You served as
a pro tern judge for the Three Lakes Judicial
District on approximately
32 days during 1995.
Are you required
to file an annual statement of economic interests
(Form 721)?
CONCLUSION
You are not required to file an annual statement of economic
interests,
since you did not serve as
a pro tern judge for four
hours or more a day for at least
30 days during 1995.
1!
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, g, .
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2!
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J STREET, SLITE 800, SACRAMENTO. CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Mr.
Munoz
Our File No.
1—96—108
Page Two
FACTS
You served as a pro tern judge for the Three Lakes Judicial
District during 1995.
According to the memorandum you submitted
that you received from the court,
you only served four hours or
more on seven days during 1995.
ANALYSIS
The Act requires state and local public officials,
including
judges and court commissioners, to file a statement each year
disclosing their personal financial interests in order that
conflicts of interest may be avoided.
(Sections 81002(c),
87200,
et seq.)
Regulation 18724 further provides that temporary or
part—time court commissioners and pro tern or temporary judges must
file such statements if they serve or expect to serve
30 days or
more in any calendar year.
The Commission has previously advised that pro tern judges and
court commissioners may count each day in which the judge serves
four hours or more as one full day of service.
A day in which the
judge serves fewer than four hours would not be counted toward the
30-day threshold for filing statements of economic interests.
(Barton Advice Letter,
No.
1-95-410.)
Therefore,
since you served
four hours or more on only seven occasions during 1995,
you are
not required to file an annual statement.
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Dixie Howard
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

Box 890158 Teinecula, CA 92589 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1—96—108 Dear Mr. Munoz: You have requested advice concerning the conflict of interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 ! Because your inquiry is general in nature, we will treat your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c). 2 ! QUESTION You served as a pro tern judge for the Three Lakes Judicial District on approximately 32 days during 1995. Are you required to file an annual statement of economic interests (Form 721)? CONCLUSION You are not required to file an annual statement of economic interests, since you did not serve as a pro tern judge for four hours or more a day for at least 30 days during 1995. 1! Government Code Sections 81000—91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, g, . All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2! Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).) 428 J STREET, SLITE 800, SACRAMENTO. CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660 Mr. Munoz Our File No. 1—96—108 Page Two FACTS You served as a pro tern judge for the Three Lakes Judicial District during 1995. According to the memorandum you submitted that you received from the court, you only served four hours or more on seven days during 1995. ANALYSIS The Act requires state and local public officials, including judges and court commissioners, to file a statement each year disclosing their personal financial interests in order that conflicts of interest may be avoided. (Sections 81002(c), 87200, et seq.) Regulation 18724 further provides that temporary or part—time court commissioners and pro tern or temporary judges must file such statements if they serve or expect to serve 30 days or more in any calendar year. The Commission has previously advised that pro tern judges and court commissioners may count each day in which the judge serves four hours or more as one full day of service. A day in which the judge serves fewer than four hours would not be counted toward the 30-day threshold for filing statements of economic interests. (Barton Advice Letter, No. 1-95-410.) Therefore, since you served four hours or more on only seven occasions during 1995, you are not required to file an annual statement. If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact me at (916) 322—5660. Sincerely, Steven G. Churchwell General Counsel By: Dixie Howard Political Reform Consultant Technical Assistance Division
